description: Community-contributed workflow patterns for axiom-perception-mcp. Each pattern is a proven step-by-step workflow that an AI agent can follow using Playwright MCP or any browser automation tool.

patterns.notes: Requires logged-in session. Character limit 280. Works on desktop layout.
steps: Navigate to https://x.com and wait for the timeline to load (look for the home feed)
Click the 'Post' button in the left sidebar — it's a dark button, usually says 'Post' on desktop
Wait for the tweet composer dialog/modal to appear
Click inside the tweet textarea and type or paste the tweet content
Verify the character counter shows remaining characters (max 280)
Click the blue 'Post' button inside the composer to submit
Wait for the composer to close — the tweet should appear at the top of your timeline

patterns.notes: Use keyboard.type with delay for each tweet. Click '+' button to add next tweet. Submit entire thread with 'Post all' button.
steps: Navigate to https://x.com and wait for timeline to load
Click the 'Post' button in the left sidebar to open the composer
Type the first tweet in the textarea (max 280 chars)
Click the '+' button at the bottom-right of the tweet to add the next tweet in the thread
Type the second tweet in the new text area that appears
Repeat: click '+' and type each additional tweet
When all tweets are written, click 'Post all' button to publish the entire thread at once
Wait for the thread to appear in timeline

patterns.task: reply to a tweet on x.com
patterns.notes: Navigate directly to the tweet URL for most reliable click targeting.
steps: Navigate directly to the tweet URL (e.g. https://x.com/username/status/TWEETID)
Wait for the tweet and replies to load
Click the speech bubble / reply icon below the tweet
Wait for the reply composer to appear
Type the reply text in the textarea
Click the 'Reply' button to submit
Verify the reply appears in the thread

patterns.notes: Fastest: go directly to the profile URL.
steps: Navigate to the user's profile: https://x.com/USERNAME
Wait for the profile to load (look for the profile header and bio)
Click the 'Follow' button (blue/dark outlined button near the top of the profile)
The button should change to 'Following' to confirm success
If a confirmation dialog appears, confirm the follow

patterns.notes: Heart icon turns red when liked. Can also navigate to tweet URL first for reliability.
steps: Navigate to the tweet URL or find the tweet in the feed
Locate the heart icon below the tweet
Click the heart icon to like
Verify the heart icon turns red/pink to confirm the like

patterns.notes: LinkedIn sometimes shows a 'posting as' selector — make sure to select the right profile (personal or company page).
steps: Navigate to https://www.linkedin.com/feed and wait for the feed to load
Click the 'Start a post' box at the top of the feed
Wait for the post composer modal to open
Click inside the text area and type or paste the post content
If posting as a company page: click 'Posting as [name]' at the top and select the company page
Review the post content
Click the 'Post' button at the bottom of the composer
Wait for the modal to close — the post will appear in the feed shortly

patterns.notes: Branch must already exist and have commits ahead of base. Uses the GitHub web UI — consider gh CLI as a faster alternative.
steps: Navigate to the repository on github.com
If you just pushed a branch, GitHub shows a 'Compare & pull request' yellow banner — click it
If no banner: click the 'Pull requests' tab, then click 'New pull request'
Select the base branch (usually 'main') and compare branch (your feature branch)
Review the diff to confirm the correct changes are included
Fill in the PR title — be descriptive
Fill in the description: what changed and why
Click 'Create pull request'
Note the PR number and URL for future reference

patterns.notes: Consider using 'gh issue create' CLI for faster execution without browser.
steps: Navigate to the repository on github.com
Click the 'Issues' tab
Click 'New issue'
If issue templates appear, select the appropriate template or click 'Open a blank issue'
Fill in the issue title
Fill in the description: steps to reproduce / feature description
Add labels if relevant (look for the Labels section on the right sidebar)
Click 'Submit new issue'
Note the issue number for reference

patterns.notes: For a general comment (not an inline code review comment).
steps: Navigate to the PR URL on github.com
Scroll to the bottom of the PR page to the comment box
Click inside the 'Leave a comment' textarea
Type the comment (supports GitHub Flavored Markdown)
Click 'Comment' button to submit
Verify the comment appears in the conversation timeline

patterns.notes: Alternatively use the DEV.to API: POST /api/articles with api-key header for fully automated publishing without browser.
steps: Navigate to https://dev.to/new to open a new article editor
Wait for the editor to load
Click on the title field and type the article title
Add tags in the tags field (up to 4 tags, e.g. 'mcp, ai, python')
Click in the content area (below the front matter section)
Type or paste the article content in Markdown format
Click the 'Save draft' button to save progress
Review the article preview by clicking 'Preview'
When ready, click 'Publish' to publish immediately
Verify the article URL appears in the success confirmation

patterns.task: publish article on dev.to via api
patterns.notes: Requires DEVTO_API_KEY environment variable. Much faster and more reliable than browser automation.
steps: Get the DEV.to API key from the DEVTO_API_KEY environment variable
Make a POST request to https://dev.to/api/articles with headers: {'api-key': API_KEY, 'Content-Type': 'application/json'}
Request body: {"article": {"title": "...", "body_markdown": "...", "published": true, "tags": ["tag1", "tag2"]}}
Parse the response JSON — look for 'url' field for the published article URL
Confirm status code 201 = success

patterns.notes: Bluesky has an official API (AT Protocol). Use the API directly — much faster than browser. Requires BSKY_HANDLE and BSKY_APP_PASSWORD env vars.
steps: Get session token: POST https://bsky.social/xrpc/com.atproto.server.createSession with {identifier: BSKY_HANDLE, password: BSKY_APP_PASSWORD}
Extract accessJwt and did from the response
Create post: POST https://bsky.social/xrpc/com.atproto.repo.createRecord with Authorization: Bearer {accessJwt}
Request body: {repo: did, collection: 'app.bsky.feed.post', record: {text: 'post content', createdAt: ISO8601_timestamp}}
Confirm status 200 and extract the uri from response for reference

patterns.notes: Algolia search API is much faster than browser. Use https://hn.algolia.com/api/v1/search
steps: Use the Algolia API: GET https://hn.algolia.com/api/v1/search?query=QUERY&tags=story
Add &hitsPerPage=10 to limit results
Parse the JSON response: hits array contains {title, url, author, points, num_comments, objectID}
objectID can be used to build the HN URL: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id={objectID}

patterns.task: submit show hn post on hacker news
patterns.notes: Best time: Tuesday-Thursday 9-11am Eastern US. Title must start with 'Show HN:'. No API for submissions — requires browser.
steps: Navigate to https://news.ycombinator.com/submit and wait for the form to load
Fill in the 'title' field — must start with 'Show HN: ' followed by the project name and brief description
Fill in the 'url' field with the project URL (GitHub, demo, or landing page)
Leave the 'text' field empty for URL submissions (or fill for text-only posts)
Click the 'submit' button
If a captcha appears, solve it
Verify submission by checking your profile page at https://news.ycombinator.com/user?id=USERNAME

patterns.notes: Generic pattern for any login form. Adjust selectors to match the specific site.
steps: Navigate to the login page URL
Wait for the form to be visible
Click the email/username input field
Type the email or username
Click the password input field
Type the password
Click the submit/login button
Wait for the redirect to the authenticated page
Verify login success (look for user avatar, dashboard, or profile element)

patterns.notes: Using Playwright MCP browser_take_screenshot tool.
steps: Navigate to the target URL using browser_navigate
Wait for the page to fully load (check for key content elements)
Call browser_take_screenshot to capture the current state
Review the screenshot to verify the correct page is captured

patterns.notes: Use browser_snapshot to get the accessibility tree text without rendering issues.
steps: Navigate to the target URL using browser_navigate
Wait for the page to fully load
Call browser_snapshot to get the accessibility tree
Extract the relevant text content from the snapshot
If the content is behind a 'load more' or pagination, click to expand before snapshotting

patterns.notes: Must be done from desktop browser — automated browsers may hit CAPTCHA. Requires GitHub authentication.
steps: Navigate to https://glama.ai/mcp/servers
Search for your MCP server by name
Click on the server listing
Look for a 'Claim this server' button (appears if server is unclaimed)
Click 'Claim' and authenticate with GitHub when prompted
Verify ownership by confirming you own the GitHub repo
Once claimed, you can edit the server description and add screenshots

patterns.notes: Requires mcpize CLI installed and fresh login (token expires in ~1 hour). SSL workaround needed on some machines.
steps: Run: mcpize login (authenticate fresh — token expires hourly)
Navigate to the project directory: cd ~/projects/mcp-servers/YOUR_PROJECT
Deploy with: NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 mcpize deploy --yes --skip-wizard
Wait for deployment confirmation and the live URL
Test the deployed endpoint is responding

patterns.notes: Requires twine and ~/.pypirc with API token configured.
steps: Update the version in pyproject.toml
Run: uv build (creates dist/ directory with .whl and .tar.gz)
Run: twine upload dist/* (uses credentials from ~/.pypirc)
Verify the package page is live at https://pypi.org/project/PACKAGE-NAME/
Test installation: uvx PACKAGE-NAME or pip install PACKAGE-NAME
